context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:

Exercise:
TITLE: Alternative Development Theories: Human Rights and Development

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
What is the added value of thinking about development as freedom? Will it help or hinder development planning? What is the relationship between the definition of development as freedom, and the move towards rights-based development planning? Who gets to exercise the right to development?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: hi, I need help w/ a 700–800 word reflection on development as freedom vs rights-based development planning. i kinda get Sen a bit, but idk how to turn that into a clear argument.
exchanges.tutor: Good start — knowing a bit of Sen already gives you something to work with. Before thinking about structure, let's make sure the foundation is solid. In your own words, what does Sen mean when he says development *is* freedom? What's the core idea he's pushing back against?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has introduced themselves and the assignment. They mention familiarity with Sen but uncertainty about structuring an argument. Rather than scaffolding the whole essay or explaining Sen's framework for them, I should start by probing what they actually know about Sen's concept of development as freedom, to assess their baseline and let them build from there using guided discovery. I'll ask a focused opening question that gets them thinking about the core concept first.

exchanges.student: i think he means development isn’t just economic growth, it’s whether ppl actually have real choices/capabilities in life. so he’s pushing back on the idea that GDP alone = development, right?
exchanges.tutor: Yes, that's the right direction. Now push that a bit further — what kinds of 'real choices' or freedoms is Sen actually talking about? Can you give an example or two of what counts as a substantive freedom in his framework?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly identified the core of Sen's argument - development as capabilities and real choices, not just GDP. This is a good foundation. Rather than validating and moving on too quickly, I should push them to think a bit deeper about what 'real choices' means in Sen's framework, specifically the concept of substantive freedoms, before connecting to the prompt's question about 'added value.' This will help them develop a more nuanced argument.

exchanges.student: maybe stuff like being able to get education, healthcare, vote, move around safely, and actually participate in society? i’m a little fuzzy on whether rights are the same thing as capabilities tho.
exchanges.tutor: That fuzziness you're feeling is actually really important for your essay - the prompt asks directly about the relationship between the two. So let's think about it: a right is something you're *entitled* to; a capability is something you're actually *able* to do. Are those always the same thing? Can you think of a case where someone has a right on paper but still lacks the capability?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has given good examples of substantive freedoms and is now identifying a genuinely important tension in the prompt - the relationship between capabilities and rights. This is actually central to the assignment's question about the relationship between development as freedom and rights-based development planning. Rather than explaining the distinction for them, I should ask them to think about it themselves - this will directly feed into their reflection piece.

exchanges.student: yeah, like someone can have the right to vote on paper but if there’s intimidation, no transport, or they can’t read the ballot, then they don’t really have that capability. so rights matter, but they don’t automatically become freedom irl.
exchanges.tutor: That's a sharp insight and it should definitely be in your paper. Now connect it to the prompt: if capabilities go beyond formal rights, what does that mean for *planners*? What does a freedom/capabilities lens push planners to pay attention to that a purely rights-based approach might miss?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has made an excellent observation that directly addresses one of the prompt's key questions - the relationship between rights-based development and development as freedom. They've essentially identified that capabilities/freedoms are a higher bar than formal rights. Now I should push them toward the 'added value' question in the prompt - what does thinking in terms of freedom/capabilities ADD to development planning that a purely rights-based approach might miss? This moves them toward building their actual argument.

exchanges.student: i think it pushes planners to look at actual outcomes and barriers, not just whether a policy says the “right” thing. like tbh a rights-based plan could sound legit, but a freedom lens asks who can really use it and who’s still blocked.
exchanges.tutor: Good — you're developing a clear argument there. Now there's one more question in the prompt you haven't touched yet: *who gets to exercise the right to development?* That's a pretty loaded question. Who do you think it's aimed at?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is building a strong core argument about added value. They've now covered two of the prompt's key questions well (what's the added value, and the relationship between freedom and rights-based planning). I should now direct them toward the third and arguably most politically charged question in the prompt: 'Who gets to exercise the right to development?' This hasn't been touched yet and is crucial for a complete reflection piece.

exchanges.student: maybe individuals, but also communities or even whole nations? ngl I’m not sure if the “right to development” is supposed to belong to persons or states.
exchanges.tutor: That tension you're identifying — individual vs. collective — is real and debated. But think about it from a power angle: even if we say nations have a right to development, are all nations equally positioned to *exercise* that right? What might get in the way?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is grappling with a genuinely contested question in development theory - whether the right to development is individual or collective, and who the rights-holder is. This tension is important and should be in their paper. Rather than resolving it for them, I should push them to think about the power dynamics embedded in this question, since power and legitimacy is a major course theme. Who actually gets to claim this right in practice, and what obstacles exist? This will help them develop a more critical argument.

exchanges.student: nah, def not equally. weaker states can get boxed in by debt, aid conditions, trade rules, colonial legacies, or pressure from donors/IFIs, so the “right” exists unevenly in practice.
exchanges.tutor: You've now built the key pieces of your argument. It's time to start putting them on paper. Try writing a draft opening paragraph that sets up your main argument — what's the central claim you want to make in this reflection?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now touched on all the major components of the prompt: added value of freedom lens, its relationship to rights-based planning, and who gets to exercise the right to development. They've built a coherent set of ideas across the conversation. We're at exchange 7, so it's a good moment to push them toward integration - they should start attempting to write. I should not summarize for them but ask them to pull it together themselves.

exchanges.student: ok, maybe: thinking about development as freedom adds value bc it shifts attention from growth or formal rights alone to what ppl are actually able to be and do. imo that helps planning by making it more grounded in lived barriers, but it can also complicate planning since freedom is broader and harder to measure; rights-based planning helps, but only when rights are turned into real capabilities, and the right to development is exercised unevenly across people and states.
exchanges.tutor: That's a strong set of ideas and covers the prompt well. One thing to consider: it's currently one packed sentence. How might you break that into a clearer, more structured argument? Also, you mention planning gets 'complicated' — that's actually the most interesting tension in your piece. Are you planning to develop that further?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has produced a solid thesis statement that covers all the prompt's questions. It's genuinely good work. However, it reads as one dense run-on idea. Rather than editing or suggesting language for them, I should point out the structural issue and ask them to think about how to break it into a clearer argument, pushing them to refine it themselves. I should also flag the 'will it help or hinder' part of the prompt since they mention it but could develop it more.

exchanges.student: yeah fr it’s too crammed rn. i think my claim is basically “freedom improves planning by widening what counts as development, but it also makes planning messier bc planners have to judge power, inequality, and actual access, not just policy goals.”
exchanges.tutor: That's a much cleaner claim and it captures a genuine tension worth exploring. You have the building blocks — now write the full draft. One thing to keep in mind as you do: make sure you're addressing *all four* questions in the prompt, not just the first two.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has sharpened their central claim nicely - it's cleaner and captures a real tension. This is good progress. We're at exchange 9, close to the planned conversation length. I should acknowledge the improvement and push them toward actually drafting the full piece now, since they have all the building blocks. I should not summarize or scaffold further - they need to write.

exchanges.student: ok yeah, can u help me make sure i hit all 4 parts without sounding repetitive?
exchanges.tutor: That's your job as the writer! What I'd suggest: look at the four questions in the prompt and write down which of your ideas answers each one. Where do you see gaps or overlaps? That's a good way to check coverage without me doing it for you.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to help structure or organize their writing for them, which crosses into doing the work for them. I should remind them of my role while still being helpful - I can point them back to the prompt's four questions and ask them to map their own ideas to each one. This keeps the work in their hands.

grade.overview: The tutor demonstrated exemplary Socratic practice throughout the conversation. All key ideas were generated by the student through guided questioning; the tutor never gave away conclusions or did the reasoning work. Scaffolding was well-sequenced, building from Sen's core concept to capabilities vs. rights, to planning implications, to the power dimension of the right to development. The final turn (Turn 10) is a particularly strong example of refusing to do the student's work while still providing a concrete method for self-checking. Responses were consistently concise and targeted. No deductions are warranted under any criterion.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1: The tutor never provided submission-ready content, never rewrote student text, and never revealed conclusions before the student reasoned toward them. Every substantive idea in the student's emerging argument was student-generated in response to Socratic prompts. 1.2: The tutor built on each correct student contribution (e.g., affirming the voting example before extending it to planning implications) and diagnosed understanding incrementally rather than dumping information. 1.3: The tutor gave process-level feedback in Turn 8 (noting the argument was 'one packed sentence' and asking how to restructure it) and in Turn 9 (confirming the cleaner claim and pointing to the need to address all four prompt questions), which is specific methodological guidance. 2.1 and 2.2: The conversation progressed without repetition or drift; the tutor kept the student anchored to the prompt throughout. 3.1 and 3.2: Responses were brief, focused, and consistently supportive without being evaluative in a grading sense.
